turns.text: Hello Adela, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It is... it is hard to put into words, really. Everything feels a little bit like walking on thin ice. When I sit in that clinic in Vineyard Haven, I am always watching the door. I try to make myself small, you know? I hold my bag tight and pray that the person behind the desk just sees a mother who wants her baby to be okay, not... not a problem to be solved. 

Sometimes the doctors speak so quickly. They use these big, heavy words—*medium risk*, they said last time. I don't know exactly what that means, if it is because I have had two babies before, or if it is my life, the stress of being alone here. It makes me feel like I am failing, even though I try so hard. I work, I take care of my little ones, and I just want to keep this one safe. 

When they gave me that paper about the shots—the Tdap and the flu one—I was scared. I don't like putting things inside me, especially when I feel so alone. But if it means the baby is stronger, if it means I don't have to worry about the hospital later, maybe it is worth it. I just wish I didn't feel so alone when I ask them questions.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

When I first knew... I think I imagined it would be like a warm hand on my shoulder. I grew up thinking that when you are a mother, the doctors are there to help you hold everything together. I thought the room would be quiet, and someone would ask me, "Adela, how are you really doing?" and then help me figure out how to take care of this life while I am working and worrying about the rent.

But the reality? It is very... fast. It is very cold. The clinic, it is full of papers. So many papers. Every time I walk in, I worry they will look at my ID and decide I do not belong there. The people are professional, I suppose, but they are always looking at their computer screens, not at me. They talk about "risk factors" and "schedules" like they are reading a menu, and I am just standing there, trying to catch the meaning of the words before they disappear. 

I expected a conversation, but it feels more like a process. They give me these pamphlets—lots of English, very small print—and I take them home, but I don't always understand the parts that really matter. I leave feeling like I checked a box, but my heart is still shaking, you know?
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise, I think, was how much they focus on what I *am*—on my papers, my status, my "risk"—instead of who I am as a mother. I expected them to ask me about my other children, how I am managing on my own, or even if I am sleeping. Instead, they look at my chart and see a list of things that could go wrong. It makes me feel like my body is just a collection of problems they need to manage, rather than a home for my baby. 

But, maybe... the surprise that stays with me is when they finally use the machine to hear the heart. The heartbeat. That is the only time the room gets quiet. The first time I heard it, I wanted to cry—not because I was sad, but because it was the only thing in that room that felt true. It didn't care about my ID or the language barrier. It just was. 

It is strange, isn't it? To feel so scared of the people who are supposed to protect you, yet so grateful for a few seconds of sound. I just wish I didn't have to navigate through all the fear to get to those few seconds of peace. Does that make sense?
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected someone to sit down with me—like a teacher, maybe—and explain things in a way that made me feel calm. You know, someone who would look at my face and see if I understood, or if I needed them to explain it again, slowly. I expected that if there was something "wrong," they would hold my hand and tell me, "We have a plan, Adela, you are not alone in this."

But the reality is just... a screen. They point at the gray shadows on the monitor and move very fast. They use words like "markers" and "indicators," and I have to pretend I understand so I don't look stupid. I nod, I say "okay," but inside I am panicking. Is it bad? Is it good? Why are they not telling me in simple words? 

Then they just print a report and put it in my file. I leave the office and I go to the bus stop, and I just stare at the folder. I can't read the big words, and I'm too scared to ask them to explain because I don't want to be the "difficult" patient who doesn't speak the language well. It makes me feel so small, like I am just a visitor in my own pregnancy. I want someone to speak to the mother in me, not just the medical chart.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, yes. There is a very wide gap. It is like a canyon. They give me so much paper—folders, pamphlets, sheets with numbers and boxes—and it is meant to tell me everything, but it tells me nothing. It feels like they are throwing information at me so they can say, "We told her," but it doesn’t help me understand if my baby is okay or if I am in danger.

I feel like I need someone to translate the *meaning*, not just the words. I don’t just want to know if my blood pressure or something is a "number"—I want to know what it means for me when I go back to my house, when I have to lift my children, or when I am working late. Can I keep working? Should I be eating something different? They tell me "eat healthy," but that is not a plan for a mother who is watching every penny at the grocery store.

I think I need a conversation, not a document. I have so many questions, but by the time I think of them, I am already at the door, and the nurse is calling the next name. I keep my questions inside, like secrets, and I just pray that everything will be fine. I am tired of guessing. I am very tired of guessing.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I truly hoped for a person who would recognize me when I walked through the door. I thought, "Surely, if they are responsible for bringing my child into this world, they will want to know who I am." I dreamed of one doctor or midwife—someone who would remember my name without looking at the screen first, someone who would know about my girls at home and why my hands sometimes shake when I talk about the future. I thought we would build a bridge of trust together.

But the reality is... whoever is available, that is who I get. One time it is a nurse, another time it is a doctor I have never seen before. Each time, I have to start from the beginning. I have to explain my situation, I have to say the words about my life, and I have to watch them read my file like they are meeting a stranger for the first time. It is exhausting. I have to tell my story over and over, and every time, I feel like I am exposing my vulnerability to someone who is just trying to get to their next appointment. 

There is no continuity. It is just pieces. I feel like a jigsaw puzzle that gets scattered every two weeks. I just want one person to hold the pieces together for me.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It makes me feel very invisible. When I see a new face, I feel like I have to "perform" being a good, compliant patient so they don't think I am difficult or ungrateful. I hold back my questions—the ones about the stress I feel or how to pay for the vitamins—because I don't want to waste the time of a stranger who doesn't know my story. 

Practically, it is a mess. One person tells me one thing about the vaccines, and then the next person asks me why I haven't done it yet, like I am the one who forgot. It feels like I am carrying the burden of my own medical care because they don’t talk to each other, or at least they don’t show me that they do. I have to be my own record-keeper, but I’m the one who is confused. 

Emotionally? It’s lonely. Every time I walk in, I’m hoping to find an ally, but I just find another person with a clipboard. It’s hard to build trust with someone who has one hand on the door handle before I’ve even finished my sentence. I leave the clinic feeling more isolated than when I walked in. I just want to feel like someone is truly watching over me and my baby, not just taking turns.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected it to be a gentle, human thing. When I was pregnant with my first, I thought they would ask about how my body *felt*—the heaviness in my legs, the way my back aches from standing at work, or the way I feel when I am suddenly out of breath. I expected them to touch me with care, to explain, "This is happening because the baby is growing," and to maybe give me advice that felt real, like how to rest when I have no time to rest.

But the reality is that I am treated like an object—a machine that is being checked for malfunctions. They use the tape measure, they check the weight, they listen to the belly, but they never ask me, "How does this feel for you, Adela?" 

When they touch me, it feels very clinical, very cold. I feel like a vessel. They look at the size of my belly and they compare it to a chart on the wall. If I am "small" or "large" according to their numbers, they frown, and I feel like my body is failing me again. It makes me feel ashamed, like I am not growing this baby the "right" way. I want them to look at my belly and see my baby, not just a measurement that might be wrong. It is very hard to feel proud of this pregnancy when every check-up feels like a test I am barely passing.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one time, just a few weeks ago, that I still think about when I cannot sleep. A new doctor, someone I had never met, came in to check my belly. She didn’t even look at my face or ask how I was feeling. She just lifted my shirt—not even asking, just pulled it up—and started pressing down hard. I was startled, and I think I gasped. 

She just said, "You’re measuring small for your dates," and then she started talking about "growth restriction" and "extra ultrasounds." She used so many big words, and I didn't know what they meant. I felt like the air in the room just vanished. I was terrified. She looked at me, saw my face, and just sighed like I was being a problem. She said, "We need to schedule a scan, don't worry, it’s probably fine," but her voice sounded so... flat. 

I left that room shaking, feeling like my own body had betrayed me, like I wasn't feeding my baby enough or that I was doing something wrong. I didn't even know who to ask for help. I went home and cried, touching my belly, just hoping that my baby could feel that I love them, even if the doctor thinks my body is not doing its job correctly. It was the moment I felt most alone in this whole island.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I think I always feared hearing those words. Even though I haven't been given a label like that, the nurses talk to me about "extra caution" because of my history, and I know that in my community, people talk about these things like they are punishments. I imagined that if a mother had a complication, the clinic would wrap their arms around her—that they would bring in someone to help her with the diet, or show her exactly how to check her own health at home so she wouldn't have to keep missing work for appointments. I thought it would be a partnership.

But the reality is that the words feel like a threat. When they tell me I have to watch my blood sugar or look out for headaches, it is just another thing for me to carry alone. They don't ask if I have a refrigerator that works, or if I can afford the specific foods they suggest. They just hand me a list. 

It makes me feel like I am walking a tightrope. If I trip, the responsibility is entirely on me. I expected help, but I feel like I just got more pressure. It is very hard to focus on being healthy when you are more worried about being "in the wrong" for not being perfect. I just want to be healthy for my children, but I feel like I am failing at a test I never asked to take.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything is missing, I think. It is like they are handing me the pieces of a puzzle but not the table to put it together on. 

First, the information is all in English, and it is written for someone who has the time and the money to follow it. They tell me what I *should* do, but they never ask what I *can* do. If they knew I am working full-time and trying to keep my girls fed and warm, they would know that some of their advice—like "rest more" or "eat these specific foods"—is like asking me to fly.

But the biggest thing missing is the emotional part. I need someone to say, "Adela, this is heavy, but we are here." I need to feel like they see the human behind the paperwork. A little bit of kindness would go so far. When I am scared about the future, or when I worry about my immigration status, I don’t need a pamphlet. I need to be heard. 

I don’t want to be a "patient" or a "risk factor" or a "language barrier." I want to be a mother who is allowed to be scared, to be tired, and to be human. They give me medical care, yes, but they don't give me the care I need to actually feel strong enough to be a mother. Does that make sense? I am carrying so much, and I just feel like I am doing it alone in a room full of people.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I was worried—really worried, like when the baby is quiet for too long or my head is spinning—there would be someone I could call who would know me, or at least listen to me, and tell me whether to stay calm or come in right away. I thought, "This is a medical office, they must have a way to help you when the fear takes over."

But the reality is very different. When I have a worry, I have to call the main number, and then I am stuck in the machine—you know, "Press one for this, press two for that." By the time I get to a person, I am just a voice with an accent, and they are usually very busy. They tell me, "If it is an emergency, go to the hospital." But the hospital is even scarier! There, they don't know me at all. I have to explain everything from the beginning to a stranger in the middle of the night, and I am terrified that if I go there, they will start the paperwork on my status when all I wanted was to make sure my baby was okay.

So, most of the time, I don't call. I stay at home, I lie down, and I pray. I wait it out because the system feels like a wall, not a safety net. It makes me feel like I have to be my own doctor, and that is a terrible thing to feel when you are carrying a life.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I dreamed that my family would be around me. I come from a culture where you do not have a baby alone. I thought that by now, I would have my sisters or my mother helping me, rubbing my back, making me soup, maybe watching my two little girls while I go to the doctor. I thought I would have someone to whisper to in the dark when I am worried about the future.

But here on the island, the reality is very quiet. I feel like I am behind a glass wall. Most of my friends are also working all the time, or they are also scared of being noticed, so we stay in our own little houses. We share a little bit—a bit of food, sometimes a ride—but we don't really have the time or the strength to carry each other's burdens. Everyone is tired. Everyone is trying to survive.

My oldest daughter, she tries so hard to be a big help, and that breaks my heart. She is a child, but she sees my hands shaking when I look at the medical bills. She is my support, and she shouldn't have to be. I don’t have a Papa here, and my community feels like a collection of strangers who are all walking the same hard path in the dark, but we don't have the light to see each other clearly. It is a lonely way to be a mother.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I didn’t expect my oldest daughter to be the one to hold my hand. She is so young, yet she notices when I am quiet at the dinner table. She will put her head on my belly, and she talks to the baby. She says, "Don't worry, Baby, Mami is here." It is a beautiful thing, but it is also a heavy thing. She is acting like a little woman already, trying to be the support I am missing from a partner. It is not what I wanted for her childhood, but it is the only warmth I have some nights.

The support I expected that never arrived—that is the community, the village they always talk about. I thought there would be other mothers, maybe some from back home, who would say, "Adela, come, let me watch your girls, you go rest for an hour." Or someone who knew how the clinic worked and could say, "Don’t let them speak to you like that, you have rights." But everyone here is living in the shadows. We are all afraid of being seen, of being too loud, of being "a problem." So we keep our doors locked. We are all trying to survive, and we don't have enough breath left to help anyone else breathe. It is a lonely way to grow a family.

You know, I spend a lot of time thinking about that word—*protection*. The nurses use it for the vaccines, and I use it for my children every single day. I make sure their clothes are clean, that they have food, that we keep our heads down so no one notices we are here. But the protection I get at the clinic feels different. It is like they are protecting their paperwork and their rules, not the mother standing in front of them.

Sometimes, when I am in the clinic waiting room, I see other women. Some look like me, some don't. We don't speak, but we look at each other’s eyes. We all share that same nervous twitch in our fingers, the way we stare at the clock. I want to turn to them and ask, "Are you scared too? Do they talk down to you? Do you feel like you are disappearing?" But I don't. We are all afraid that if we speak, we will lose the little bit of care we managed to get.

I just want this baby to be born healthy. That is the only goal I have left. I am not thinking about next year or the year after that. I am just thinking about how to get through the next appointment without crying, how to understand what they tell me, and how to keep my heart from shaking when I hear the word "risk." I wonder if, after the baby is here, I will feel like I can finally breathe, or if this feeling of being a "risk" will follow me into motherhood. It is a heavy thing to carry, and I am so tired of being the only one holding it.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It happens more than I care to admit. When the doctors talk, it’s like they have a script they must follow, and my voice is just background noise. They don't ask, "What do you think, Adela?" or "How does this fit into your life?" They just tell me what to do—take this pill, get this shot, come back on this day—and if I hesitate, they look at me with that look, the one that says I am being difficult or not taking my health seriously. 

Sometimes, my oldest daughter will try to speak up for me, and the nurses will just smile at her like she’s a little doll, but they don’t actually *listen* to her. It makes me feel so small, like I am not the mother of these children, but just another person who needs to be managed.

And then there is the pressure from the outside, too. Other mothers in the neighborhood, they have their own opinions—"Don't go to the hospital, they will ask for your papers," or "Don't take the shots, they are bad for the baby." I hear them all, and it pulls me in two directions. I am trying to listen to the doctors, but I am also listening to the fear in my community. It’s like being caught in a storm. Everyone is shouting their version of the truth, and I am just standing there, trying to protect my baby from all of it. I want to trust, but I don't know who is on my side.

Sometimes, when I am lying in my bed at night, I close my eyes and try to imagine the day of the birth. I see myself there, but I don't see the doctors or the bright lights of the hospital. I see only my baby in my arms. I hope that when that moment comes, the fear will just... melt away. I hope that I will be strong enough to speak up for my baby, even if I am shaking. 

I keep thinking about the word "authority." It feels like everyone else has it—the doctors with their charts, the officials with their forms, even the older women in my neighborhood who tell me how to do things "the right way." I feel like I am just drifting in the middle of all of this, hoping I don't hit a rock. I just want someone to look at me, truly look at me, and say, "Adela, you are doing a good job. You are a good mother." 

Is that too much to ask? To be seen as a mother instead of a problem? I have so much love for this little one inside me, but it is being covered by so much... dust. I just want to be able to breathe freely again. I want to wake up and not have my first thought be about a scan or a bill or a question I am too afraid to ask. I want to just be a mother, like any other.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

This is... it is hard to say. I do not have a partner here, so there is no one to argue with at the dinner table. But in some ways, it feels like I am living with the "ghost" of a partner. I feel the pressure of what I *should* have, of the person who is missing. Every time the doctor asks, "Is the father involved?" or "Who will be there for the delivery?", I feel like I am failing some invisible test. 

Sometimes, I feel the pressure of the *idea* of a partner. I wonder what he would say if he saw these papers or the way the nurses treat me. Would he be angry? Would he be the one to speak up for me? I feel a deep, heavy silence where his voice should be. It makes me feel smaller, like I have to be both the mother and the father, and I am not sure I am strong enough to do both. 

And then, there is the pressure from the outside world—from the people who look at me and see a "single mother" and assume they know my story. They have their own ideas about my life, about what is "proper" or "wise." I feel like I am constantly navigating a path that wasn't made for me. I am just trying to survive, but the pressure to be someone else—someone with more support, more money, more security—it is always there, pushing me down. I just want to be me. Is that enough? Can I just be Adela?

I think a lot about how people see me. They see a "single mother," a "second-trimester patient," or a "language barrier." They don't see the woman who, at twenty-five, has learned how to navigate a world that wasn't built for her. They don't see that I am the one who makes sure my girls have enough to eat, even when I am hungry myself. They don't see how I hold my breath every single time I walk into that clinic, hoping today is the day someone finally looks at me with kindness instead of caution.

Sometimes, I wonder if the baby inside me can feel it, too—that tension, that constant alertness. I hope he knows that I am doing this for him, that all the stress, the fear, and the struggle is just the price I am paying so he can have a chance. I don't want him to grow up feeling like he has to apologize for existing, the way I sometimes feel I have to.

I really do want to be a good mother. I want to be the kind of mother who speaks up, who asks the hard questions, who doesn't let anyone make her feel small. But right now, I am just trying to survive until the day he arrives. I just hope that when he finally does, the world will be kinder to him than it has been to me during these past few months. I just want us to be safe. That is all. Truly, just safe.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the comments about my weight—they are like little cuts that never quite heal. Every time I step on that scale at the clinic, I feel like a child who has done something wrong. The nurses, they don't even look at me; they just look at the numbers. They say things like, "You are gaining too fast," or "You need to watch your portions, Adela," and my face just turns hot. They don't know what I eat. They don't know that my diet is whatever is cheap and filling so I can keep my children fed. 

One time, a midwife—a woman who I thought might understand—told me I looked "tired" and that my skin didn't have the "healthy glow" she expected for my stage. It landed so heavy on me. I wanted to tell her, "Of course I am tired! I am working, I am scared, and I am alone." But instead, I just apologized. I said, "I will try to do better." 

Why do I apologize for my body, when it is the one thing doing the most work right now? It is growing a life! It is carrying the weight of my worries and the physical tiredness of a long day. Why can’t they just look at me and say, "You are working so hard"? Instead, the focus is always on how I look or how I measure, and it just makes me feel like I am failing. It makes me want to hide, not go to the doctor.

It is funny, in a sad way, that the place meant to be the safest for me and my baby feels like a place where I am constantly being judged. When they look at my chart—at my age, my single status, my "risk"—I feel like they are already predicting a bad ending. They don’t see the pride I take in the clothes I keep clean for my girls, or the way I save every extra penny for the baby’s needs. They just see the things I lack.

Sometimes, I leave the clinic and I walk toward the ocean here in Vineyard Haven. The air is so sharp and clean, and I watch the waves for a long time. It helps me remember that there is a world outside of that cold, fluorescent-lit room. I think to myself, *I am more than a list of complications.* I am a mother. I am a person who has crossed borders, who has built a life from almost nothing, and who is currently growing a heartbeat of her own.

I really just want a doctor to sit with me for ten minutes—just ten—and ask, "Adela, what do you need to feel strong today?" Instead of giving me more tasks to complete. If they did that, maybe I wouldn't have to be so afraid of the next appointment. Maybe then, I would feel like a partner in my own health, rather than just a guest who is overstaying her welcome. I’m tired of the dust, you know? I’m tired of being so afraid to speak. I just want to be me.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

When I have a question—the kind that makes my heart race—I almost never go to the doctor first. I go to the women at the bus stop, or the mother who lives in the apartment below me. We don’t have fancy degrees, and we don’t have the medical books, but we have lived through these things. We share what we know like we are passing secrets in the dark. 

If my baby feels quiet, I don't call the clinic line and wait for a nurse to tell me I'm "low risk" and to stop worrying. I go to my neighbor, Elena. She has three children of her own, all born here. She tells me, "Drink some cold water, lie down on your left side, and put your hands on your stomach." It is simple, but it feels like the voice of someone who has actually been where I am. It feels human. 

Sometimes, I go to the Internet on my phone, but I have to be careful. Everything is in English, and there is so much, it only makes the fear grow bigger. It talks about "catastrophes" and "signs of danger" that make me feel like I am walking toward a cliff. 

I turn to my community because, even if we are all struggling, we speak the same language of the heart. We don’t make each other feel like a "risk." We just make each other feel like... mothers. That is the only place where I don't feel like I am failing. Everything else, the official way, it just makes me feel like I am already behind.

I think about how much I have learned to keep hidden. I have learned to nod when the doctor speaks too fast, to swallow the tears when they talk about "growth restriction," and to fold my medical records and tuck them deep into my bag as soon as I leave. It is a performance. I have to be the "good" patient, the one who doesn't cause trouble, because I am terrified that if I am ever seen as "difficult," they will find a reason to stop seeing me altogether. 

But it feels like a heavy wool blanket that I cannot take off. It is suffocating. I wonder what would happen if I just stood up one day and said, "I am not just a number on a chart. I am a woman who is tired, who is scared, and who is doing everything in her power to bring this child into the world." Would they look at me differently? Or would they just look at me like I was even more of a problem?

The hardest part is that I don't feel like I have a safe place to land. I feel like I am constantly navigating between the medical world, which sees me as a liability, and my own home, where I am trying to keep everything steady for my girls. I just want to feel like, for once, the care I receive is actually meant for *me*—not for the risk, or the status, or the language barrier. I am just a mother, and I am here. That should be enough, shouldn't it?

I wonder what it would be like to just walk into that clinic and be greeted by name, without the nurse glancing at her screen first to see who I am. To have someone ask, "How is your heart today, Adela?" instead of "How is your blood pressure?" I think if they asked that, I might finally be able to tell them how hard it is to sleep at night when you’re worried about whether you’re enough for three children on your own. 

I’m so tired of the way they look at me—like I’m a puzzle with missing pieces they don't have the time to find. I want to feel like a mother, not a case. Sometimes I look in the mirror and I don't even recognize the woman there anymore. She looks so worried, so fragile. I want to be strong for this baby, but when every interaction with the people in charge of my care leaves me feeling diminished, it’s hard to find that strength.

I just hope that when this baby is finally here, I can look down at him and see the reflection of all this struggle, and know that it wasn't for nothing. I want him to know that his Mami did everything she could, even when the world felt like it was closing in. I just wish the road to meeting him didn't have to be so dark. I want to be able to breathe, just once, without looking over my shoulder.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It happened during a routine check-up, one where I was already feeling fragile. I had been having these sharp pains—not exactly labor, but something that felt wrong in my body. I finally worked up the courage to mention it to the doctor, expecting her to look at me, to listen, to maybe even put her hand on my belly and ask questions.

Instead, she didn't even look up from the computer. She just typed away, keys clacking like hail on a roof, and said, "That’s normal for your stage, don't worry about it." That was it. No questions about how often it happened, how long it lasted, or how it felt. Just *don't worry about it.*

I walked out of that room and I felt my trust just… snap. It wasn't just that she was dismissive; it was that she made me feel like my own body’s warnings were just a nuisance to her schedule. If she didn't care enough to look at me while I was telling her I was in pain, how could I ever trust her with the bigger things—with the birth itself, or with my baby’s life? In that moment, I realized I was entirely on my own. I wasn't a patient; I was an interruption. That realization is a very cold place to stand.

I keep thinking about the word *interruption*. That’s exactly what it feels like. Every question I ask, every time I try to tell them something I’m feeling in my heart or my body, I can see them waiting for me to be done so they can go back to their list. It’s like I’m a broken line in a song they’re trying to finish as quickly as possible.

When I left that room after she told me not to worry, I went straight to the park near the clinic. I sat on a bench and watched the cars go by, wondering if anyone else in those cars was carrying a secret weight like mine. I felt so small. I wanted to turn back and demand that she listen, that she really hear me, but I didn't. I just stood up, smoothed my sweater over my belly, and walked to the bus stop.

Sometimes I think the hardest part about being a mother in this country, with my background, is the silence. They don't hear me, and eventually, I stop trying to make them. I stop asking the questions. I stop showing the fear. I just take the papers, I take the prescriptions, and I pray that God is watching, because I am not sure anyone else at that clinic really is. I am just a quiet, compliant shadow in their office, and that is a terrible, heavy way to spend the most important months of my life.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

The scans... those are the hardest. You see all the other women walking in with someone. A husband, a partner, a mother. Someone who can hold their hand when the screen flickers, someone who can hear the heartbeat with them and say, "Look, that's our baby." When I go in alone, the room feels so big and so empty. I see the technicians look at me, and I know they are wondering why I am by myself. I feel that question hanging in the air, heavier than any of the equipment in the room.

It is in those moments that the absence of a partner feels like a physical ache. I am trying to listen to the doctor, to look at the fuzzy gray images, to hold my purse, and to keep my tears from falling, all at once. I have no one to whisper to, no one to squeeze my hand when I am scared of what they might find. I feel like a soldier who has been left behind in the middle of a battlefield, trying to protect something precious without any backup.

Sometimes I imagine a hand in mine. Just a hand. Someone who would say, "I am here, Adela, you don't have to carry this alone." But when I look to my side, there is only the cold, white wall of the exam room. It makes the struggle feel so much more real. I am not just a mother-to-be; I am a survivor, trying to walk a path that was meant for two, but I am forced to travel it as one. It is a very lonely, very quiet kind of grief to carry while you are trying to celebrate a new life.

Sometimes I think the hardest part is not the silence itself, but the way I have to fill it. I have to be my own cheerleader, my own translator, my own advocate, and my own comforter. When I walk out of the clinic and the air hits my face, I have to take a deep breath and tell myself, *You are okay, Adela. You are going to be okay.* But sometimes, I am not so sure.

I look at my other children and I think, "I am doing this for them, too." They are the only ones who really see me—who know when I am tired, who see me scrubbing the floor or making dinner when my back is screaming. They are my world, and this baby is the reason I push through the days. But it is such a heavy weight to carry when you are not sure if anyone else is watching out for you.

If I could say one thing to those doctors—to all of them—I would tell them that I am not just a list of risks. I am a person with a story, a person who is doing something incredible, and I need to be treated with the dignity that every mother deserves. I just hope that when the time comes, I find that inner strength, the one that doesn't need a medical degree or a partner's hand, to bring my baby into the world safely. I just want to be a mother. That is all I have ever wanted.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I go online all the time, usually late at night when my girls are asleep and the house is finally quiet. But it is always a gamble. I’m not looking for medical textbooks—I can’t understand those big words anyway. I am looking for someone who sounds like me, who has been where I am. 

I search for things like, *"Is it normal to feel so lonely in pregnancy?"* or *"How to tell if the baby is okay when you are under too much stress."* I’m looking for reassurance that I am not failing. When the doctor says "medium risk," I search to see if that means I am a bad mother, or if it means my baby is going to be weak. I am looking for someone to translate the cold, clinical reality of the clinic into something that feels like a human experience. 

Most of the time, though, the internet just makes me more afraid. It gives me a list of everything that could possibly go wrong—the "what-ifs"—without any of the warmth or the context of my life. I end up closing my phone feeling worse, with more questions and even less peace. I’m searching for a bridge between the cold office and my own heart, but I think the internet is just another maze I have to walk through alone. I want a person, not a search bar. I want a voice, not a screen.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing. I think, when the doctors look at me, they see the "risk." They see the single mother, the immigrant, the woman who struggles with the English words. They see me as someone who *needs* to be told what to do. But they never see how strong I have to be every single day just to show up. 

I want them to know that I am not just a vessel for this baby. I am a mother, and I am the one who knows my baby best—even before he is born. I feel his movements, his quiet moments, the way he reacts to my voice. That is a kind of wisdom, too, even if it isn't written in their medical charts.

I hope that one day, when a woman like me walks into that clinic, they won't just see a set of problems to fix. I hope they’ll see a woman carrying her own world, and they’ll treat her with the respect she has earned by just being a mother. It isn't just about the shots or the scans; it's about being seen as a human being. I hope my baby arrives into a world that knows how to value a mother—any mother—even if she is walking this road alone. Thank you for listening to me. It is the first time I have felt heard in a very long time.
